mission.title: Install and Configure Kubevirt on Kubernetes
mission.description: KubeVirt is a virtual machine management add-on for Kubernetes that allows you to define and manage virtual machines alongside other Kubernetes resources. Installing KubeVirt enables you to leverage Kubernetes for virtualization, providing a unified platform for managing both containerized and virtualized workloads.

steps.title: Add the KubeVirt Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the KubeVirt Helm repository to your local Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kubevirt https://charts.kubevirt.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KubeVirt using Helm
steps.description:
Install KubeVirt in the 'kubevirt' namespace (it will be created if it doesn't exist) with the following command:
```bash
helm install kubevirt kubevirt/kubevirt --namespace kubevirt --create-namespace --version v1.7.0
```

steps.title: Verify KubeVirt installation
steps.description:
Check that all KubeVirt components are running by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubevirt
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for KubeVirt components
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, you can configure resource limits for KubeVirt components. Create a YAML file named `kubevirt-resources.yaml` with the following content:
```yaml
apiVersion: kubevirt.io/v1
kind: KubeVirt
metadata:
  name: kubevirt
spec:
  configuration:
    developerMode: false
    resourceLimits:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "4Gi"
``` 
Then apply the configuration:
```bash
kubectl apply -f kubevirt-resources.yaml -n kubevirt
```

steps.title: Enable monitoring for KubeVirt
steps.description:
To enable monitoring, you can install Prometheus and Grafana. Use the following command to install them:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubevirt/kubevirt/master/deploy/monitoring/monitoring.yaml
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KubeVirt will show all pods in the 'kubevirt' namespace as running when you execute `kubectl get pods -n kubevirt`. You will also have configured resource limits and enabled monitoring for your KubeVirt installation.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubevirt https://charts.kubevirt.io
helm repo update
helm install kubevirt kubevirt/kubevirt --namespace kubevirt --create-namespace --version v1.7.0

apiVersion: kubevirt.io/v1
kind: KubeVirt
metadata:
  name: kubevirt
spec:
  configuration:
    developerMode: false
    resourceLimits:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "4Gi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove KubeVirt, first uninstall the Helm release with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall kubevirt --namespace kubevirt
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After removing the release, clean up any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created. Execute the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd virtualmachines.kubevirt.io
kubectl delete crd virtualmachineinstances.kubevirt.io
kubectl delete crd virtualmachineinstancesets.kubevirt.io
kubectl delete crd virtualmachinepresets.kubevirt.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'kubevirt' namespace and verify its removal:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubevirt
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading KubeVirt, back up the current state of your resources. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get kubevirt -n kubevirt -o yaml > kubevirt-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the latest version of KubeVirt:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kubevirt kubevirt/kubevirt --namespace kubevirt --version v1.8.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that all KubeVirt components are running correctly:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubevirt
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If KubeVirt pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs of the affected pod to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kubevirt
``` 
Look for error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: KubeVirt components not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If KubeVirt components are not starting, check the events in the namespace for any errors:
```bash
kubectl get events -n kubevirt
``` 
This will help identify any resource constraints or configuration issues.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you suspect that resource limits are causing issues, check the resource usage of the KubeVirt pods:
```bash
kubectl top pods -n kubevirt
``` 
Adjust the resource limits in `kubevirt-resources.yaml` if necessary and reapply the configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Monitoring not working
troubleshooting.description:
If monitoring is not functioning as expected, verify that the Prometheus and Grafana pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubevirt
``` 
Check the logs of the Prometheus pod for any errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <prometheus-pod-name> -n kubevirt
```

prerequisites.description: You need a running Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
